audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for flogging/mock kingship, Pilate, behold the human, no king but Caesar, crucifixion, mother/beloved disciple, thirst/finished, blood/water, burial.

verses.restored: Then therefore Pilate took Jesus and flogged him.
The soldiers, weaving a crown from thorns, placed it on his head and threw a purple garment around him.
They were coming to him and saying, Hail, king of the Judeans, and giving him strikes.
Pilate went outside again and says to them, Look, I bring him out to you so that you may know I find no cause in him.
Jesus therefore came out, wearing the thorn crown and purple garment. Pilate says to them, Look, the human.
When therefore the chief priests and officers saw him, they cried out, saying, Crucify, crucify. Pilate says to them, You take him and crucify, for I find no cause in him.
The Judeans answered him, We have a Law, and according to the Law he ought to die, because he made himself Son of the Divine.
When Pilate therefore heard this word, he was more afraid.
He entered the praetorium again and says to Jesus, From where are you? But Jesus gave him no answer.
Pilate therefore says to him, You do not speak to me? Do you not know that I have authority to release you and authority to crucify you?
Jesus answered him, You would have no authority against me unless it had been given to you from above; because of this the one handing me over to you has greater sin/misalignment.
From this Pilate was seeking to release him, but the Judeans cried out, saying, If you release this one, you are not Caesar's friend; everyone making himself king opposes Caesar.
Pilate therefore, hearing these words, brought Jesus outside and sat on the judgment seat at a place called Stone Pavement, and in Hebrew Gabbatha.
It was Preparation of the Passover, about the sixth hour. He says to the Judeans, Look, your king.
They therefore cried out, Take away, take away, crucify him. Pilate says to them, Shall I crucify your king? The chief priests answered, We have no king except Caesar.
Then therefore he handed him over to them so that he might be crucified. They took Jesus.
Carrying the cross for himself, he went out to the place called Skull Place, which is called in Hebrew Golgotha.
There they crucified him, and with him two others, on this side and that side, and Jesus in the middle.
Pilate also wrote a title and placed it on the cross. It was written: Jesus the Nazarene, the King of the Judeans.
Many of the Judeans read this title, because the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city; and it was written in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek.
Therefore the chief priests of the Judeans said to Pilate, Do not write, The King of the Judeans, but that he said, I am King of the Judeans.
Pilate answered, What I have written, I have written.
The soldiers therefore, when they crucified Jesus, took his garments and made four parts, a part for each soldier, and the tunic. The tunic was seamless, woven from above through the whole.

They said to one another, Let us not tear it, but cast lots for it, whose it will be, so that the Scripture might be fulfilled: They divided my garments among themselves, and for my clothing they cast lots. Therefore the soldiers did these things.
Standing by the cross of Jesus were his mother, his mother's sister, Mary of Clopas, and Mary Magdalene.
Jesus therefore, seeing the mother and the disciple whom he loved standing by, says to the mother, Woman, look, your son.
Then he says to the disciple, Look, your mother. And from that hour the disciple took her into his own.
After this, Jesus, knowing that all things had already been completed, so that the Scripture might be completed, says, I thirst.
A vessel full of sour wine was set there; therefore putting a sponge full of sour wine around hyssop, they brought it to his mouth.
When therefore Jesus received the sour wine, he said, It has been completed. And bowing the head, he handed over the spirit.

Therefore the Judeans, since it was Preparation, so that the bodies might not remain on the cross on the Sabbath, for that Sabbath was a great day, asked Pilate that their legs might be broken and they taken away.
Therefore the soldiers came and broke the legs of the first and of the other crucified with him.
But coming to Jesus, when they saw he had already died, they did not break his legs.
But one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and immediately blood and water came out.
The one having seen has borne witness, and his witness is true; and that one knows that he says true things, so that you also may trust.
For these things happened so that the Scripture might be fulfilled: Not a bone of him will be broken.
And again another Scripture says: They will look on the one whom they pierced.

After these things Joseph from Arimathea, being a disciple of Jesus but hidden because of fear of the Judeans, asked Pilate that he might take the body of Jesus; and Pilate permitted it. He came therefore and took his body.
Nicodemus also came, the one who first came to him by night, bringing a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about a hundred litrai.
They took the body of Jesus and bound it in linen cloths with the spices, as is burial custom among the Judeans.
In the place where he was crucified there was a garden, and in the garden a new tomb in which no one had ever yet been laid.
There therefore, because of the Judean Preparation, because the tomb was near, they laid Jesus.
